The   Trial   Master   shall   be   allowed   actual   and   reasonable   expenses   and   shall   be   paid   out  of Grand Lodge funds upon approval of the Grand Master.

OFFENSES   AND   TRIALS   111  Section   1.   APPOINTMENT.  CHAPTER 9  TRIAL   COMMISSION  The Grand Master shall not later than thirty (30) days after the close of the Annual  Communication   appoint   sixteen   (16)   brothers   as   Trial   Commissioners.   Eight   of   these   shall  be   from   the   eastern   half   of   the   State   and   eight   from   the   western   half   of   the   State.   They   shall  serve without pay except for actual and necessary expenses when called.  Section   2.   ASSIGNMENT.  Whenever   a   trial   by   Trial   Commission   is   required   or   asked   for   as   provided   by   law,   the  Trial   Master   shall   summon   four   of   the   Trial   Commissioners   from   the   half   of   the   State   where  the   trial   is   to   be   held.   The   Trial   Commission   shall   consist   of   the   Trial   Master   and   four   Trial  Commissioners, none of whom shall be members of the lodge of which the accused is a  member. The Commission shall have jurisdiction to try the accused.  Section   3.   SECRETARY.  The   Commission   shall   select   its   own   Secretary,   who   may   be   the   Secretary   of   the   lodge  of   the   accused.   Said   Secretary   shall   perform   the   duties   of   the   Secretary   defined   in   Chapter  7, Section 6 of Offenses and Trials.  Section   4.   TRIAL.  The   trial   shall   then   be   conducted   by   the   Commission   in   the   lodge   room   of   the   lodge   of  the accused or such other lodge designated by the Trial Commission, in the same manner  and under the same   regulations in all   other respects   as   if the   trial were being conducted in  open lodge except as hereinafter set forth.  The   Commission   may   summon   witnesses,   fix   the   time   and   place   and   make   all   the   neces -  sary   arrangements   for   the   proper   conduct   of   the   trial.   It   shall   determine   the   guilt   or   innocence  of   the   accused,   voting   by   secret   ballot.   Four   members   shall   concur   to   convict,   and   the   Trial  Master shall have a vote thereon.  Section   5.   PENALTY.  If   the   accused   is   found   guilty,   the   Commission   shall   fix   the   penalty   in   accordance   with  the   provisions   of   Chapter   15,   Section   3   of   Offenses   and   Trials   and   shall   promptly   deliver   a  full   and   complete   report   of   its   action   in   the   case,   together   with   a   transcript   of   all   testimony  heard and proceedings had therein, to the Grand Secretary.  Section   6.   COMPENSATION.  The   Trial   Commission   shall   be   allowed   actual   and   reasonable   expenses   and   shall   be   paid  out of Grand Lodge funds upon approval of the Grand Master.

OFFENSES   AND   TRIALS   112  Section   1.   HOW   MADE.  CHAPTER 10  APPEARANCE  In   proceeding   upon   a   charge   of   unmasonic   conduct,   the   appearance   of   the   accused   shall  be in one of the following ways:  a.   By   Personal   Presence.   By   his   personal   presence   at   the   time   and   place   of   which   he  has had notice.  b.   By   Counsel.   By   the   presence   of   any   Master   Mason   in   good   standing   who   files   with  the Secretary written authority of the accused to appear for him.  c.   By   Written   Communication.   By   the   written   communication   of   the   accused,   or   his  authorized   counsel,   filed   with   the   Secretary   at   any   time   before   or   at   the   stated   communica -  tion at which he is cited to appear.  Section   2.   JURISDICTION.  An appearance in any of the ways provided above shall give to the lodge jurisdiction,  notwithstanding any   defect in   or want   of service of   notice as   provided in Chapter   7   of   Of -  fenses and Trials.  Section   3.   FAILURE   TO   APPEAR.  a.   Personal Service.   In case of personal service, if the accused fails to appear, such  failure shall be deemed equivalent to a confession of guilt as charged, and the lodge shall  proceed to punish as upon a plea of “guilty.”  b.   Substituted Service.   In case of substituted service, if the accused fails to appear,  the Trial Master shall appoint a discreet brother as counsel for the accused. It shall be the  duty of such counsel to preserve to the accused every legal and equitable right under the  law.   In   such   case,   if   an   objection   to   a   charge   is   not   sustained,   a   plea   of   “not   guilty”   must   be  entered by the counsel or by order of the Trial Master.  CHAPTER 11  OBJECTION   —   PLEA  Section   1.   OBJECTION   OR   PLEADING.  A brother appearing in a proceeding against him for unmasonic conduct must either  object to each charge or plead thereto.  Section   2.   OBJECTION   DEFINED.  An   objection   is   a   written   statement   signed   by   the   objector,   his   counsel,   or   by   a   member  of the lodge, and filed with the Secretary to the effect that he objects to the charge of the  information.   In   effect,   an   objection   is   a   claim   that   the   statements   of   the   charge,   even   if   true,  do not constitute a triable Masonic offense or that the lodge does not have jurisdiction.  Section   3.   FORM.  The   objection   may   be   in   the   following   form:  The   undersigned   objects   to   charge   No.   (or   charges   Nos.)   of   the   information.  If   there   is   more   than   one   charge,   each   one   objected   to   must   be   specified   by   number.

OFFENSES   AND   TRIALS   113  Section   4.   DISPOSAL.  The Trial Master shall either sustain or overrule every objection filed. If he finds that  the charge does not state a triable Masonic offense or that the lodge has no jurisdiction to  try the brother, he must sustain the objection. If he finds that a Masonic offense is stated  and that the lodge has jurisdiction, he must overrule the objection. A   separate ruling must  be made on each charge objected to.  Section   5.   CHARGE   DISMISSED.  If an objection is filed and sustained, the charge should be dismissed by order of the  Trial Master unless the complainant will at once amend it so as to state an offense triable  by the lodge.  Section   6.   PLEA   DEFINED.  A   plea   is   an   answer   to   the   charge   or   charges.   It   is   either   “guilty”   or   “not   guilty.”   It   may   be  in   writing   signed   by   the   accused   or   by   his   counsel,   or   may   be   orally   stated   by   either   of   them  to   the   Secretary.   The   Secretary   must   enter   the   plea   in   substantially   the   following   form:   “Now  comes the accused   and to charge No.   pleads   ‘guilty,’”  or “now comes the accused   and to charge No.   pleads  ‘not guilty.’” No particular form of plea is necessary, but, unless the accused clearly and  unequivocally pleads “guilty,” a plea of “not guilty” must be entered for him. In case of  substituted service see Chapter 10, Section 3b of Offenses and Trials.  Section   7.   PLEA   REQUIRED.  If the objection is overruled, the accused must at once plead to the charge unless for  good reason the time is extended by vote of the lodge.  Section   8.   PLEA   ENTERED.  a.   Personal   Service.   In   case   of   personal   service   or   of   the   appearance   of   the   accused,   if  no   objection   to   the   charge   or   charges   is   made,   or   if   made   and   overruled,   the   accused   should  plead   to   each   charge.   If   he   fails   to   plead   or   pleads   “guilty,”   no   trial   is   necessary.   The   Trial  Master orders the plea of “guilty” to be entered, and punishment follows.  b.   Substituted Service.   If the service is substituted and the accused fails to appear,  the Trial Master must appoint counsel as provided in Chapter 10, Section 3b of Offenses  and   Trials.   Such   counsel   may   object   and   if   his   objections   are   overruled   must   enter   a   plea   of  “not guilty.”  Section   9.   FORMER   CONVICTION   OR   ACQUITTAL.  Former   conviction   or   acquittal   of   the   offense   charged   need   not   be   specially   pleaded.   It  may be shown under a plea of “not guilty.”  Section   10.   JUDGMENT.  If   the   accused   is   found   guilty,   either   upon   his   plea   of   “guilty”   or   his   failure   to   plead   as  provided,   the   Trial   Master   shall   order   record   made,   in   substance,   as   follows:   “The   accused  is   adjudged   guilty,   on   his   own   admission,   of   the   charge   of   the   information,”   or   “of   the   first  or second,” as the case may be.  Section   11.   PUNISHMENT.  If   a   plea   of   “guilty”   is   entered,   then   punishment   follows   as   provided   by   law.   If   the   law  fixes the punishment, no vote is to be taken.

OFFENSES   AND   TRIALS   114  CHAPTER 12  TRIALS   AND   SUMMONSES  Section   1.   REQUIRED   FOR   TRIAL.  There   shall   be   no   trial   of   any   charge   until   all   objections   made   are   disposed   of   and   issue  is taken by the entry of a plea of “not guilty.”  Section   2.   TRIAL   LODGE.  If   a   plea   of   “not   guilty”   is   entered,   the   Master   must   summon   a   trial   lodge   for   the   purpose  of   trying   the   issue.   The   communication   shall   be   special   and   held   at   such   time   as   the   Master  deems   expedient.   However,   if   the   Master   does   not   summon   a   trial   lodge   within   thirty   (30)  days after a plea is filed, the Trial Master shall summon it within ten days thereafter.  Section   3.   FORM   OF   SUMMONS.  The   summons   with   the   seal   of   the   lodge   attached   may   be,   in   substance,   as   follows:  , Iowa,   ,20  To Brother   :  Take   notice,   that   a   special   communication   of   Lodge  No.   ,   A.F.   &   A.M.,   is   ordered   to   convene   at   its   hall   on   the   day  of   , 20   , at   o’clock P.M., to consider and pass upon   charges  preferred against Brother   , at which time and place your attendance  is required.  (Seal)  ,   Secretary  Section   4.   SERVICE   OF   SUMMONS.  a.   On   Whom.   The   Secretary   shall   summon   all   members   of   the   lodge   in   good   standing,  except   those   not   entitled   to   vote   who   are   hereby   debarred   from   being   present   in   a   trial   lodge.  b.   How.   The summons may be served on members personally or by forwarding the  same by mail to them at their last known place of residence.  c.   By Whom.   Service shall be made by the Secretary. One certificate will cover all  services made. It   need not state how service was   made   on any one   member   and   may be   in  the form set out below.  d.   Return   of   Service.   The   return   of   the   Secretary   as   to   such   service   should   be   substan -  tially in the following form:  I hereby certify that I summoned the members of   Lodge  No.   , A.F. & A.M., to attend the special communication of said lodge to be held on  the   day of   , 20   , by serving each member personally with   a  written notice of the time, place, and purpose of such communication, or by forwarding  such notice by mail addressed to him at his last known place of residence.  ,   Secretary  Section   5.   PROSECUTOR.  After   an   issue   is   formed,   the   Trial   Master   may   appoint   any   Master   Mason   to   prosecute  the   charges.   The   appointee   may   be   a   member   of   the   particular   lodge   or   of   another   lodge   in  this   Grand   Jurisdiction.   In   the   absence   of   such   appointment,   the   duty   shall   devolve   upon   the  Junior   Warden.   Such   prosecutor   shall,   in   the   discharge   of   his   duties,   keep   steadily   in   view  the welfare of the Craft and the attainment of a just result.

OFFENSES   AND   TRIALS   115  Section   1.   HOW   TAKEN.  CHAPTER   13  TESTIMONY  Testimony for use on the trial   may be given in open lodge at the time of the trial, or it  may   be   taken   before   a   committee   of   three   or   less,   who   shall   be   Master   Masons   appointed   by  the   Trial   Master.   The   Trial   Master   may   appoint   one   or   more   committees   at   any   time,   giving  such   directions   and   making   such   changes   as   emergencies   may   require,   the   purpose   being   to  obtain   the   testimony   with   fairness   to   both   parties.   The   Trial   Master   should   note   the   duty   to  be   performed   and   select   the   committee   with   care.   Aside   from   rulings,   the   duties   are   largely  clerical, and a committee of one will work to better advantage.  Section   2.   COMMITTEE   AND   LODGE.  A   part of the testimony may be taken before a committee and a part in open lodge, as  the   Trial   Master   shall   decide.   In   case   witnesses   cannot   be   produced   in   lodge,   their   testimony  must be taken before a committee.  Section   3.   WHO   PRESENT.  During   the   taking   of   testimony   before   a   committee,   it   is   the   right   of   the   accused   and   his  counsel,   or   either   of   them,   to   be   present   and   cross - examine   the   witnesses   for   the   lodge   and  to   examine   his   own   witnesses,   who   may   be   cross - examined   by   the   prosecutor   for   the   lodge.  The   prosecutor   may   have   present   to   assist   him   any   person   or   persons   whose   presence   is   not  Masonically prohibited. No one shall be present during the taking of testimony unless he  has a duty to perform in connection therewith.  Section   4.   TIME   AND   PLACE   OF   MEETING.  When   testimony   is   taken   by   a   committee,   the   committee   shall   fix   the   time   and   place   and  give   reasonable   notice   to   the   prosecutor   for   the   lodge   and   to   the   accused   or   his   counsel.   At  the time set, it will be the duty of the respective parties to produce their witnesses.  Section   5.   TESTIMONY   BY   A   MASON.  Testimony   given   by   a   Mason   shall   be   on   his   honor,   by   an   affirmative   answer   to   an   inter -  rogatory addressed to him by the Trial Master or chairman of the committee, as follows:  “Do you solemnly promise, on the honor of a Mason, that the testimony you shall give in  the case now pending shall be the truth?”  Section   6.   TESTIMONY   BY   A   PROFANE.  Testimony   by   a   profane   shall   be   under   oath   administered   by   a   person   authorized   by   state  law.  Section   7.   RULINGS   ON   ADMISSIBILITY.  If the committee to take testimony consists of only one member, he shall rule on the  admissibility   of   the   testimony   offered.   If   the   committee   consists   of   more   than   one,   the   Trial  Master, in appointing such committee, shall designate one as chairman, who shall make  such   rulings.   On   the   trial   such   rulings   shall   be   made   by   the   Trial   Master,   but   the   record   shall  show all questions, the answers to which are excluded either by the committee or by the  Trial Master.

OFFENSES   AND   TRIALS   116  Section   8.   OBJECTIONS   TO   EVIDENCE.  Either   party   may   at   any   time   object   to   any   question   or   answer,   and   no   ground   of   objection  need   be   stated,   but   merely,   “I   object   to   the   question,”   or   “answer,”   and   then   it   may   be   argued  at   the   pleasure   of   the   committee.   The   chairman   shall   sustain   or   overrule   the   objection,   keep -  ing in mind that in cases of doubt it is safer to admit than to   exclude, because if improper  the testimony may afterwards be disregarded. If excluded wrongfully, it may necessitate  taking testimony again. This admonition, however, should not lead the committee to too  great liberality, and thus burden the record with clearly improper testimony.  The   rulings   should   be   written   down   in   the   minutes,   but   no   exceptions   are   necessary   to  have them reviewed on appeal. See Chapter 16, Section 2 of Offenses and Trials.  Section   9.   REPORT   OF   COMMITTEES.  a.   Required.   Each committee appointed to take testimony shall reduce the same to  writing, as nearly in the language of the witness as practicable, and report   the same to the  lodge   at   the   time   fixed   for   trial.   If   not   so   reported,   the   committee   shall   state   the   reason   why,  and the lodge in its discretion may postpone the trial and grant further time.  b.   Form.   The form   of report shall   be   substantially   as   set   out   in the   Official Form   for  the   Committee   to   Take   Testimony.  Section   10.   TAKEN   IN   OTHER   AREAS.  In case testimony is to be taken in other areas in this State, it shall be the duty of the  Master,   Wardens,   and   Secretary   of   the   lodge   in   the   area   where   the   testimony   is   to   be   taken,  or   any   of   them,   at   the   instance   of   the   Trial   Master,   to   act   as   a   committee   to   take   such   testi -  mony, and of the trial lodge to make reasonable compensation for such service.  Section   11. REDUCED   TO   WRITING.  The   testimony   must   be   reduced   to   writing   and   as   near   as   may   be   in   the   language   of   the  witness.   As   the   evidence   of   each   witness   is   completed,   it   should   be   signed   by   him.   However,  failure to do so will not exclude it.  Section   12.   CERTIFICATE OF   COMMITTEE.  When all the witnesses are examined, the committee shall append a certificate to its  report as set out in the Official Form for the Committee to Take Testimony.  Section   13.   PRESERVED.  The testimony taken shall be preserved, the purpose being that all the testimony used  on the trial shall become a part of the record.  Section   14.   EVIDENCE.  a.   Records.   On the trial of any   Mason accused   of a Masonic   offense also punishable  under the laws of the United States or those of any state or territory thereof, the record of  his   conviction   in   a   Federal   court   or   a   court   of   any   state   or   territory   of   the   United   States,   or   a  certified   copy   of   such   conviction,   shall   be   competent   evidence   and   prima   facie   proof   of   guilt.  However, evidence upon a Masonic trial shall not be limited to such record of evidence.  b.   Former   Conviction   or   Acquittal.   The proof   of   former   conviction or   acquittal is   the  original   record   of   the   proceeding   in   which   such   conviction   or   acquittal   was   had   or   a   certified  transcript   thereof.   If   such   record   is   not   obtainable,   other   proof   may   be   given.   When   proven,  such conviction or acquittal is a bar to further proceedings.

OFFENSES   AND   TRIALS   117  Section   15.   WITNESS   FEES.  No   fees   or   compensation   shall   be   paid   to   witnesses   who   are   Masons   to   attend   and   give  evidence   before   a   committee.   It   is   their   duty   so   to   do   at   the   time   and   place   fixed   as   heretofore  provided.   Expenses   paid   by   a   witness   for   the   lodge   may   be   paid   by   the   lodge.   As   to   profanes,  each   party   shall   procure   his   evidence   as   best   he   can,   the   expense   whereof   each   party   must   bear.  CHAPTER 14  TRIAL   PROCEDURE  Section   1.   ON   WHAT   DEGREE   OPENED.  When   the   lodge   is   convened   for   trial   purposes,   it   shall   be   opened   on   the   highest   degree  to   which   the   accused   has   attained,   and   the   testimony   shall   then   be   taken   or   completed   unless  already completed before committees appointed for that purpose.  Section   2.   NUMBER   OF   COMMUNICATIONS.  The trial lodge should be confined to one communication if possible. If this is impos -  sible, the Master may close the lodge to open at a definite time and date which the Trial  Master shall designate. The date should be the earliest practicable. In such case the lodge  must be closed and not called to refreshment. There may be as many communications as  are necessary to complete the trial.  Section   3.   ATTENDANCE.  a.   Only   members   of   the   trial   lodge   who   are   entitled   to   vote   and   other   Masons   who   have  a   duty   to   perform   in   connection   with   the   trial   may   be   present   thereat.   It   is   the   duty   of   every  member   present   at   the   first   communication   to   attend   each   subsequent   communication.   Only  those present at the entire trial shall be permitted to vote.  b.   After   the   commencement   of   the   trial,   no   brother   shall   be   admitted   to   the   lodge,   except  the   accused   or   his   counsel   without   the   consent   of   the   accused   or   his   counsel   and   that   of   the  prosecutor   for   the   lodge.   However,   members   admitted   after   the   trial   begins   should   not   vote  on any question before the trial lodge.  c.   No   member of   the   lodge   authorized to   vote   shall   be   excused from the   lodge   by the  Trial   Master   except for pressing necessity.   A   mere temporary   absence during which   the trial  may be suspended is not an absence within the meaning of this section.  Section   4.   TESTIMONY   BEFORE   COMMITTEE.  The   preceding   section   does   not   apply   to   the   taking   of   testimony   before   a   committee.  Section   5.   RULES   FOR   TAKING   TESTIMONY.  In   taking   testimony   at   the   trial,   the   same   rules   shall   govern   as   in   the   case   before   a   com -  mittee. The Trial Master shall preside and rule upon all questions. If the testimony or any  part thereof was taken before a committee, it shall be read in the lodge.  Section   6.   EXCLUSION   OF   TESTIMONY.  If   improper   testimony   has   been   allowed   before   a   committee,   either   party   may   at   trial   ask  to   have   it   excluded, by   which   is   meant   not   considered, and   such question must   be   decided  by   the   Trial   Master.   No   reason   need   be   given   in   making   the   request.   Counsel   may   thereupon  suggest   any reasons   they have   for or against   it. When informed,   the   Trial   Master will say,  “The request is granted,” or “refused,” as he may judge proper.

OFFENSES   AND   TRIALS   118  Section   7.   RECORD.  If any part of the testimony is suppressed, there should be no erasure on the record of  such   testimony,   but   the   record   of   the   proceedings   should   show   the   action   taken.   Suppressed  testimony should be neither read nor considered on the trial.  Section   8.   VARIANCE   FROM   ALLEGATIONS.  The   fact   that   there   is   a   difference   between   the   time   or   place   as   alleged   and   proved   will  not   defeat   the   action.   The   time   is   not   material,   nor   is   the   place,   except   that   it   should   not   be  so stated as to mislead.  Section   9.   OATH   NOT   REQUIRED.  When   the   lodge   has   assembled   for   the   trial   and   has   been   opened   as   provided   in   Section  1   of   this   Chapter   no   oath,   promise,   or   pledge   is   necessary   to   qualify   the   members   to   act   as  a trial lodge, the covenants of a Master Mason being sufficient for qualification.  Section   10.   ACCUSED   AS   WITNESS.  The   lodge   cannot   call   the   accused   as   a   witness,   but   the   accused   may   elect   to   be   a   witness  in   his   own   behalf.   In   such   case   the   prosecution   can   examine   him   fully   as   to   all   the   particulars  of the case, whether touched upon in his examination or otherwise, and the lodge shall be  the sole judge of his credibility and the weight to be given to his evidence.  Section   11. CROSS - EXAMINATION.  Legal   limitations   as   to   cross - examinations   have   no   application   in   Masonic   trials.   When  a   witness   is   once   introduced,   either   party   may   seek   any   information   such   witness   possesses  relative to the issue on trial.  Section   12.   SECRET   WORK.  In   case   the   production   of   testimony   should   disclose   any   part   of   the   esoteric   (unwritten)  work   of   Freemasonry,   the   lodge   or   the   committee   shall   omit   the   same   from   the   written   record  of the case, and the same, if used, must be given orally at trial.  Section   13.   ARGUMENT.  After all the testimony is in, the prosecutor and the accused, or his counsel, shall be  heard   if   they   so   desire.   The   prosecutor   shall   have   the   right   to   open   and   close   the   argument.  The lodge may limit the time for such argument.  Section   14.   DELIBERATION.  When the argument is concluded, the accused and his counsel shall retire unless the  counsel   is   a   member   of   the   lodge.   If   the   lodge   is   not   then   open   on   the   Third   Degree,   it   shall  be so opened for deliberation and action. In the course of deliberation the members may  speak and seek such   information as   they   desire, observing therein the will   and   pleasure   of  the Trial Master.  Section   15.   VOTING   ON   THE   QUESTION   OF   GUILT.  a.   Method.   After due   deliberation, the   Trial Master shall submit to the   lodge   the   fol -  lowing   question   as   to   each   charge   under   investigation:   “Is   the   accused   guilty   or   not   guilty?”  In answer to the question each   member   shall   deposit his   ballot with the words   “Guilty” or  “Not   Guilty.”   A   majority   vote   of   “guilty”   shall   convict,   and   the   fact   must   be   entered   on   the  record, the following language in substance being used: “The accused is adjudged guilty

OFFENSES   AND   TRIALS   119  of   the   offense   charge,”   or,   if   the   information   contains   more   than   one   charge:   “The   accused  is adjudged guilty of the offense set out in Charge No.   ,”   giving   the   number   or  numbers.  b.   Excused.   No member of the lodge present and qualified to vote, except counsel,  shall be excused from voting on the question of guilt or punishment.  Section   16.   ACQUITTAL.  If the vote of “guilty” is less than a majority, the accused is acquitted of the offense  charged on which the vote is taken. The fact of acquittal must be entered on the record,  the following language in substance being used: “The accused is acquitted of the offense  charged,”   or, if the information contains   more than   one charge: “The accused is acquitted  of the offense set out in Charge No.   ,” giving the number or numbers.  Section   17.   PUNISHMENT.  If there is a conviction on one or more of the charges, the lodge must then proceed to  impose punishment as provided in the following chapter.  Section   1.   DEFINED.  CHAPTER 15  PUNISHMENTS  The   punishments   which   may   be   imposed   on   conviction   for   unmasonic   conduct   are:  a.   First   –   Expulsion.  b.   Second   –   Suspension.  c.   Third   –   Reprimand.  Section   2.   PRESCRIBED.  a.   Expulsion.   In   case   of   conviction   of   any   of   the   offenses   described   in   Chapter   3,   Sec -  tions 1, 2, 3, 4, or 8 of Offenses and Trials the accused shall be expelled.  b.   Declared   by   Trial   Master.   In   case   the   punishment   is   prescribed   by   law   (see   Subsec -  tion a above) the Trial   Master, without a vote of the lodge, shall make declaration thereof  and order the declaration made of record. This constitutes the sentence which shall be in  force until altered by the Grand Lodge.  Section   3.   EXPULSION   OR   SUSPENSION   BY   VOTE.  In   case   of   conviction   where   no   punishment   is   prescribed   by   law,   the   lodge   shall,   by  a majority vote, determine what the punishment shall be. The vote thereon shall be by  written ballot, and for that purpose the Trial Master shall first inquire: “Shall the brother  be expelled?” In answer to such question the vote shall be taken, and those favoring such  punishment shall deposit a ballot with the word “yes,” and those opposed shall deposit a  ballot   with   the   word   “no.”   If   there   is   a   majority   for   expulsion,   the   Trial   Master   shall   declare  the   accused   expelled,   which   fact   shall   be   entered   of   record   and   stand   as   the   judgment   of   the  lodge.   If   less   than   a   majority   vote   for   expulsion,   the   Trial   Master   shall   then   inquire:   “Shall  the   brother   be   suspended?”   and   in   answer   thereto   the   same   proceeding   shall   be   had.   If   there  is a majority vote for suspension, the Trial Master shall declare the accused suspended.

OFFENSES   AND   TRIALS   120  Section   4.   REPRIMAND.  a.   When Given.   If there is not a majority vote for suspension, then without further  action   by   the   lodge,   the   Master   shall   proceed   to   administer   a   reprimand.   If   the   presence   of  the   accused   is   obtainable,   the   reprimand   shall   be   administered   at   the   same   communication.  If not, the Master shall fix an early day for such purpose. The Secretary shall notify the  brother   to   appear   at   such   communication,   and   when   he   appears,   the   Master   shall   administer  the reprimand.  b.   When in Writing.   In special cases, at the discretion of the Master, and where the  presence of the accused cannot reasonably be required, the reprimand may be in writing  and   forwarded   to   the   accused.   In   such   cases   the   reprimand   shall   be   read   in   open   lodge,   and  a copy filed and preserved among the records.  Section 5.   PUNISHMENTS   COMBINED.  Only one punishment can be imposed under one information; that is, where there is a  conviction   on   two   or   more   charges   in   the   same   information,   the   members   in   voting   a   pun -  ishment must   have in   view   one   adequate to   all   the   offenses   of which   there is   a   conviction.  CHAPTER   16  APPEALS  Section   1.   JUDGMENT   IN   FORCE.  In all cases of conviction and punishment by a subordinate lodge, or expulsion by the  order   of   the   Grand   Master,   the   judgment   shall   be   in   force   until   reversed,   set   aside,   or   modi -  fied by the Grand Lodge.  Section   2.   RIGHT   OF   APPEAL.  In   proceedings   upon   charges   of   unmasonic   conduct,   no   appeal   lies   from   the   decision   of  the Trial Lodge or the Trial Commission except by the accused.  No   exceptions   need   be   taken   by   the   accused   to   any   ruling   or   other   proceeding   connected  with the trial in order to protect his rights on appeal. On appeal the entire record may be  reviewed, and on such review, except where there is substantial prejudice to the rights of  the accused, all technicalities, errors, and illegalities are to be disregarded. However, the  Grand Lodge shall not render a more unfavorable judgment against the accused.  Section   3.   TIME   LIMIT.  Every Mason entitled to appeal to the Grand Lodge under the provisions of Section 2  of   this   Chapter   may   do   so   within   thirty   (30)   days   after   the   rendition   of   the   verdict,   or   letter  of   expulsion,   suspension,   or   reprimand,   but   not   thereafter.   Record   of   the   appeal   is   made   by  order   of   the   Trial   Master.   See   Chapter   4,   Section   2d   of   Offenses   and   Trials.   See   form   notice,  Section 4 of this Chapter.  Section   4.   NOTICE.  Such   appeal   must   be   taken   by   filing   with   the   Secretary   of the   lodge   a   written   notice of  appeal, substantially in the following form:

OFFENSES   AND   TRIALS   121  To   , Secretary of   Lodge No.   ,   A.F. &   A.M.:  You   are   hereby   notified   that   the   undersigned   appeals   to   the   Grand   Lodge   of   Iowa,  A.F. & A.M., from the judgment of said lodge against him, rendered on the   day  of   , 20   .  ,   Appellant.  Section   5.   SERVICE   OF   NOTICE.  The   notice   of   appeal   shall   be   left   with   the   Secretary   who   shall   file   the   same   and   enter   it  on the record on the date of delivery. No other service is necessary. It will be well for the  appellant   to   see   that   the   notice   is   filed   on   the   date   of   delivery,   as   this   would   be   important   if  a question should arise as to whether or not the appeal was taken in time.  Section 6.   TRANSCRIPT   REQUIRED.  Whenever an appeal is taken under the provisions of this chapter, the Secretary of the  lodge   shall   forthwith   prepare   and   forward   a   complete   transcript   of   the   proceedings   includ -  ing the testimony.   In all   cases   where such transcript is   received by the Grand Secretary as  early   as   the   Friday   preceding   the   regular   communication   of   the   Grand   Lodge,   the   case   shall  be for disposition at such communication unless continued at the instance of the accused  or   of   the   Grand   Lodge.   If   received   after   such   time,   the   case   shall   be   for   disposition   at   such  communication only by consent of the accused and the Grand Lodge.  The   Secretary   shall   keep   an   exact   record   of   the   proceedings   in   the   order   and   at   the   time  of   their   occurrence,   and   the   form   for   that   purpose   should   be   as   set   out   in   the   Official   Form  for Transcript.  Section   7.   NO   APPEAL   —   TRANSCRIPT   NOT   REQUIRED.  After   a   verdict   of   guilty,   or   letter   of   expulsion,   suspension,   or   reprimand,   as   provided   in  Chapter   4,   Sections   2a   and   2b   of   Offenses   and   Trials,   the   proceedings   can   only   be   reviewed  in the Grand Lodge on appeal by the accused. If no appeal is taken, no transcript need be  sent to the Grand Secretary.  Section   8.   SECRET   WORK.  If on appeal to the Grand Lodge testimony given under the provisions of Chapter 14,  Section   12   of   Offenses   and   Trials   shall   be   material,   any   brother   who   heard   the   testimony   at  the   trial   may   be   a   witness   before   the   Grand   Lodge   or   its   committee   as   to   what   such   testimony  was, but in no case shall it be reduced to writing.  Section   1.   REVERSAL.  CHAPTER   17  RETRIALS  When   a   case   is   reversed   for   error   in   the   proceedings   of   the   trial   lodge   which   are   of   sub -  stantial   prejudice   to   the   rights   of   the   accused,   or   for   failure   to   file   with   the   Grand   Secretary  a   proper   transcript   of   the   record,   the   Grand   Lodge   may   order   the   case   remanded   for   retrial  or for such correction of the transcript as may be required.  Section   2.   NOTICE.  If a   retrial is   ordered,   the Grand Secretary shall   promptly   notify the trial lodge   of   such  action   and shall   attach to   his   notice   a   copy   of the   report   adopted by   the   Grand Lodge.   The

OFFENSES   AND   TRIALS   122  trial   lodge   shall   bring   the   case   on   for   retrial   within   a   reasonable   time   from   the   receipt   of  such notice.  Section   3.   RULES.  Such   retrial   shall   be   governed by   the   provisions   of   the   Code   relating   to   trials   in   subor -  dinate   lodges.   The   charges   and   plea   thereto   made   on   a   former   trial   may   stand   as   the   charges  and plea on the retrial unless the Grand Lodge or Trial Master shall order such charges  amended   or   changed.   If   amended,   new   charges   shall   be   served   on   the   accused   as   provided  by   law.   Either   party   may   introduce   any   or   all   competent   evidence   introduced   at   the   former  trial and such additional evidence as may be competent.  Section   4.   AFTER   APPEAL.  After   final   disposition   of   any   matter   pending   in   the   Grand   Lodge   on   appeal,   or   in   which  its   jurisdiction   was   original,   a   rehearing   may   be   granted   upon   application   by   a   party   thereto,  if such showing shall be made that the Grand Lodge believes such rehearing to be in the  interest of justice.  Section   5.   RETRIAL — SUBSTITUTED   SERVICE.  In   case   the   service   is   “substituted   only”   where   there   is   no   appearance   by   the   accused   in  any   of   the   ways   provided   and   there   is   a   conviction   and   a   judgment   of   expulsion   or   suspension  and   the   accused   shall   thereafter   appear   within   six   months   from   the   date   of   conviction   and   ask  for   a   rehearing   and   take   issue   on   the   charge   or   charges   in   the   manner   provided,   he   shall   be  entitled   to   such   rehearing.   The laws   applicable   to   trials   shall   apply   thereto.   If   at   such   trial   the  accused   shall   be   acquitted,   the   judgment   of   acquittal   shall   have   the   effect   to   vacate   the   former  judgment   and   reinstate   the   accused.   If   at   trial   the   accused   shall   be   convicted   and   the   punish -  ment   imposed   be   different   from   the   former,   then   the   former   judgment   shall   stand   modified  to   conform   to   the   latter.   The   mere   fact   of   retrial   does   not   affect   the   former   judgment.   Such  judgment   is   only   affected   by the   retrial   when   the   judgment   on   retrial   is   different, and   then   only  to   conform thereto.   The   date   of   the   judgment   in   either   case   is   that   of   the   former judgment.  Section   6.   RIGHT   OF   APPEAL.  After   a   retrial   and   judgment   of   expulsion   or   suspension   as   provided   in   Section   5   of   this  Chapter,   the   accused   shall   have   the   right   of   appeal   in   the   time   and   manner   provided   in   this  Code. For the purpose of appeal, the time shall be computed   from the date of judgment at  the retrial. (Chapter 16 of Offenses and Trials)  Section   1.   APPEARANCE.  CHAPTER 18  GRAND   LODGE   TRIALS  The appearance to the information may be the same as provided in Chapter 10 of Of -  fenses and Trials for appearance in subordinate lodges, and the law as to objections and  pleas   in   subordinate   lodges   shall   obtain   in   cases   of   complaint   on   information in   the   Grand  Lodge   as   far   as   applicable.   However,   the   time   for   filing   the   same   shall   be   that   fixed   by   the  Grand Master unless the Grand Lodge shall afterwards grant further time.

OFFENSES   AND   TRIALS   123  Section   2.   PLEA.  Where there is a substituted service and no appearance, the case shall be treated both  by the Grand Master and the Grand Lodge as one with a plea of not guilty.  Section   3.   APPOINTMENT   OF   COMMITTEE.  In all cases where an issue of fact is presented by a plea of not guilty, whether such  plea is entered by the accused or by the Grand Master in case of a substituted service, the  Grand Master shall appoint a committee of from one to three. Such committee shall sit at  some   convenient   and   reasonably   inexpensive   place   to   the   parties   to   take   the   testimony.   The  Grand   Master   may   require   such   committee   to   find   and   report   to   the   Grand   Lodge   the   facts  and their conclusions of guilt or innocence based thereon.  Section   4.   REPORT   OF   COMMITTEE.  The   appointed   committee   shall,   when   its   labors   are   ended,   file   its   report   with   the   Grand  Secretary.   If   such   report   contains   only   the   testimony,   the   Grand   Secretary   shall   at   once   place  the   same   in   the   hands   of   the   Committee   on   Appeals   and   Grievances   who   shall   examine   the  same   and   report   their   findings   of   fact   and   conclusions   as   to   guilt   or   innocence   for   the   action  of the Grand Lodge. If the committee reporting the testimony also reports its findings by  direction of the Grand Master, then such report shall   be presented to the Grand Lodge for  its action without reference to the Committee on Appeals and Grievances.  Section   5.   GRAND   LODGE   NOT   IN   SESSION.  In   all   such   cases   where   the   proceedings   are   had   when   the   Grand   Lodge   is   not   in   session  and   when   the   committee   by   its   report   finds   that   the   accused   is   guilty   of   the   charges   preferred,  such   report   and   findings   shall   be   entered   of   record   in   the   office   of   the   Grand   Secretary.   From  that date the accused shall stand suspended from all the rights and privileges of Masonry  until   the next   regular communication of the   Grand   Lodge when final action shall   be   taken  on such report by the Grand Lodge.  Section   6.   ACTION.  After   the   filing   of   the   report   by   the   appointed   committee,   the   Grand   Lodge   has   full   and  complete   jurisdiction   of   the   case   and   may   review   the   testimony   and   approve   or   disapprove  the findings of   the committee   in whole or in   part.   However,   it   may refer the same   to other  committees,   it   being   the   intention   that   the   Grand   Lodge   at   its   communication   shall   have   full  discretion   and   authority   for   final   disposition   of   the   case   subject   only   to   such   limitations   as  are expressed in the law.  Section   7.   DETERMINATION.  a.   Punishment.   Punishment   in   case   of   conviction   when   the   Grand   Lodge   has   jurisdic -  tion shall be that prescribed in like case in subordinate lodge trials.  b.   Degree.   The   degree   shall be determined   in   the   same   manner   as obtains in   such  subordinate lodge trials.  Section   8.   DISCIPLINE   IN   GRAND   LODGE.  In matters of discipline of members of the Grand Lodge for unmasonic conduct at a  regular communication, whether during hours of labor or refreshment, the Grand Lodge  may proceed to try and punish the offender in a summary manner.

OFFENSES   AND   TRIALS   124  Section   9.   EXPULSION   OR   SUSPENSION.  When the Grand   Lodge has   original jurisdiction and expels   or suspends a Mason,   such  expulsion or suspension shall be from all the rights and privileges of Freemasonry unless  otherwise   expressed.   Reinstatement   in   such   case   must   be   by   a   two - thirds   vote   of   the   Grand  Lodge   on   written   petition.   A   Mason   thus   reinstated   is   not   thereby   restored   to   membership   in  any   particular   lodge   of   the   jurisdiction   but   has   the   standing   of   a   demitted   Mason.   In   case   of  an   Entered   Apprentice   or   Fellow   Craft,   the   reinstatement   shall   be   to   the   rank   he   held   before  expulsion   or   suspension   with   the   right   to   apply   for   advancement   to   the   lodge   of   his   residence.  Section   10.   TECHNICALITIES.  In case a right of action or inquiry in the Grand Lodge is given, such action or inquiry  shall   not   be   dismissed   for   the   reason   that   no   form   or   method   of   procedure   is   prescribed   by  the Code. In such case the form or method shall be discretionary with the Grand Master  who   shall   have   in   view   the   attainment   of   just   results.   It   is   the   intention   that   the   provisions   of  the   law,   in   their   essence   and   spirit,   shall   be   observed   and   that   technical   deviations   therefrom,  not   prejudicial   to   or   in   violation   of   an   absolute   right,   shall   not   defeat   the   application   of   the  law nor avoid the penalty for its infraction.  Section   11. OTHER   PROCEEDINGS.  As to instituting other proceedings in the Grand Lodge, instructions are unnecessary.  After the   proceedings   are   instituted, the   power   of the   Grand   Lodge   is   so   discretionary   that  no definite rules of procedure need be given.  CHAPTER 19  COMPLAINTS   AGAINST   LODGES  Section   1.   GRAND   LODGE   REVIEW.  a.   In case a Mason is aggrieved by the action of a lodge, whether such action is by a  decision   or   ruling   of   the   Master   on   behalf   of   the   lodge   or   by   a   vote   of   the   lodge   or   by   reason  of   a   failure   of   the   lodge   to   act,   he   may   petition   the   Grand   Lodge   for   a   review   thereof,   stating  the   facts   constituting   his   grievance.   During   the   recess   of   the   Grand   Lodge,   he   may   present  the same to the Grand Master who may, if he regards it of sufficient importance to merit  consideration,   institute   an   inquiry   as   to   the   facts   to   be   presented   to   the   Grand   Lodge,   or   the  matter   may   be   referred   to   the   Grand   Lodge   for   its   action.   In   such   case   the   complainant   shall  file a copy of his complaint with the accused lodge.  b.   The   provisions   of   this   section   are   also   applicable   to   decisions   made   by   the   Grand   Master  during the recess of the Grand Lodge. In such case the petition should be filed with the  Grand Secretary, and the Grand Master should take no preliminary steps for the hearing.  Section   2.   LODGE   DESERVES   DISCIPLINE.  a.   Any   Master   Mason,   whether   acting   upon   his   personal   knowledge   or   upon   informa -  tion,   may   complain   in   writing   to   the   Grand   Lodge   or   to   the   Grand   Master   that   a   lodge   of   the  jurisdiction   is   deserving   of   discipline   for   any   reasons   known   to   the   law.   The   reasons   are   to  be   stated   in   the   complaint   in   a   general   way.   If   the   complaint   is   to   the   Grand   Lodge,   it   may  assume jurisdiction. If it does, it may act thereon upon such   notice as it may deem just. If  filed   with   the   Grand   Master   during   a   recess   of   the   Grand   Lodge   and   he   deems   the   complaint

OFFENSES   AND   TRIALS   125  deserving   of   consideration,   he   shall   notify   the   lodge   thereof,   and   by   committee   or   otherwise  he may make inquiry into the facts for the information and action of the Grand Lodge.  b.   In such proceeding the Grand Lodge need not be confined to facts stated in the  complaint but may add others coming to its knowledge.
